segments.purgatorio-25-g38: Poi is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'afterwards' placed before the verb for temporal sequence. Cominciò is the passato remoto (third person singular) of cominciare, 'he began'; the colon marks that direct speech follows.
segments.purgatorio-25-g39: Se is the conditional/if introducer (here 'if'); le is the feminine plural definite article 'the' agreeing with parole; parole is plural 'words'/'speech' (object of se); mie is the feminine plural possessive 'my', placed after the noun in poetic style.
segments.purgatorio-25-g40: Figlio is a vocative addressing the addressee ('son' / 'my son'); its placement after a comma shows direct address and does not alter the sentence's syntactic structure.
segments.purgatorio-25-g41: La mente tua is the subject phrase ('your mind'), with article + noun + possessive (tua). Guarda e riceve are present-tense verbs (third person singular) joined by e ('and'); guarda here = 'attends to/observes' and riceve = 'receives' (i.e. accepts into understanding). The order is normal subject followed by compound predicate.
segments.purgatorio-25-g42: This clause is elliptical: lume ('light' or illumination) is the direct object; ti is the indirect object pronoun 'to you'; fiero here must be read as an archaic or dialectal future meaning 'I will make' (cf. medieval fossilized forms of 'fare' — grammatically a first‑person future used by the speaker). Al = a + il ('to the'/'on the'); come che tu die = 'the way/how that you say' where come = 'how/way', che introduces the subordinate clause and tu die is present subjunctive/indicative usage meaning 'you say' (die = 'dici' in Dantean spelling).
segments.purgatorio-25-g43: Sangue perfetto is a noun phrase with noun before adjective (common in Dante): sangue 'blood' modified by perfetto. Here perfetto means 'fully formed'/'mature' rather than a grammatical tense; the phrase names the subject of the sentence.
segments.purgatorio-25-g44: Che introduces a relative clause ('which'). Poi is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. Non si beve is a pronominal/passive construction: si + beve (present tense of bere) meaning 'is not drunk' or 'is no longer taken up'; da marks the agent or source here ('by' / 'from') and l'assetate vene = 'the thirsty veins' (vene is the feminine plural 'veins', with assetate agreeing).
segments.purgatorio-25-g45: E si rimane = 'and it remains' (si + rimane is pronominal/impersonal but here refers back to the blood). Quasi alimento = 'almost like food/sustenance'. Che di mensa leve is a relative clause: che = 'that' and di mensa leve literally 'taken from the table' (di + mensa = 'from the table'; leve is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'lifted/taken' in this context).
segments.purgatorio-25-g46: Prende is present tense (third person singular) of prendere: 'it takes' or better 'it imparts/imbues'. Nel core = 'into the heart' (in + il). A tutte membra umane = 'to all human members/limbs' (a + tutte + noun + adjective). Virtute informativa = noun phrase: virtute 'virtue' or 'power/effect' and informativa 'formative' (an adjective derived from 'informare' in the sense 'to give form'); together they mean 'a formative power' that the blood imparts.
segments.purgatorio-25-g47: Come introduces a comparison 'like' or 'as'. Quello refers back to some prior thing (the formative agent being compared). Ch’ = che ('that' / 'which') and a farsi is an infinitival construction where farsi (here reflexive/passive sense 'to be made' / 'to come into being') is governed by a; the phrase says 'which, in bringing those to be made, by means of (per) the vain/empty veins' (per le vene vane = 'through the empty/vain veins').
segments.purgatorio-25-g48: 'Ancor' is an adverb meaning 'still' and sets a temporal/background circumstance; 'digesto' is a past-participial adjective (poetic/archaic usage) meaning 'digested' or 'being assimilated' and here forms an absolute/adverbial phrase (comma-separated) that modifies the main verb that follows.
segments.purgatorio-25-g49: The finite verb 'scende' (present indicative of 'scendere') is the main action 'descends' or 'goes down.' 'ov’' is the clipped form of 'ove/ov' meaning 'where'; 'è' is the present of 'essere' ('is'); 'più' is the comparative adverb 'more' and 'bello' here is used idiomatically ('more fitting/pleasant/beautiful') to express what is preferable. 'tacer' and 'dire' are infinitives in a comparative construction with 'che' functioning as 'than' (not a relative 'that'). Together the clause means 'it descends to the place where it is preferable to be silent than to speak.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g50: 'e' = 'and.' 'quindi poscia' is a close temporal sequence: 'quindi' here functions as 'then/from there' and 'poscia' as 'afterward/then,' so together they mark the following action as ensuing from the prior one. 'geme' is the present indicative 'groans/laments.' 'sovr'' = 'sopra' contracted, 'over/upon.' 'altrui' is a possessive/adjectival pronoun meaning 'of another' (i.e. 'another person's'); 'sangue' = 'blood.' 'in natural vasello' = 'in a natural vessel' (vasello = small vessel/container), a figurative/physical container. The whole clause: 'and then afterward he laments over another’s blood in a natural vessel.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g51: 'Ivi' = 'there.' 's’accoglie' is a reflexive/passive-voice form of 'accogliere' ('is received' / 'is welcomed'); grammatically this frames a passive/impersonal reception. 'l’uno e l’altro insieme' means 'the one and the other together' (two parties received jointly). The order (verb before subject phrase) is normal poetic inversion but the sense is passive reception of both parties.
segments.purgatorio-25-g52: 'l’un' (the one) is followed by 'disposto' (past participle used adjectivally: 'disposed' or 'prepared') with 'a' introducing an infinitival purpose 'a patire' = 'to suffer' (patire = endure/suffer). The short phrase describes the state/intention of one of the two persons: 'the one prepared to suffer.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g53: 'e l’altro a fare' parallels the previous phrase: 'and the other to do/act.' 'per lo perfetto loco' uses 'per' in the sense 'through/by means of' and 'lo perfetto loco' = 'the perfect place' (adjective + noun) identifying the medium. 'onde' = 'from which' / 'by which' introduces a relative clause; 'si preme' is a reflexive/impersonal present of 'premere' (literally 'presses' or 'is pressed on'), so the clause modifies the place: 'through the perfect place from which it is pressed/urged.' The structure ties the other's action to that particular locus and its force.
segments.purgatorio-25-g54: Simple coordinating conjunction 'e' = 'and' linking the prior clause to what follows.
segments.purgatorio-25-g55: 'giunto' is a past participle used deverbally (absolute participle) meaning 'having arrived' or 'when he has come,' and 'lui' is the pronoun 'he/him.' Together they introduce a temporal clause: 'and, he having arrived,' = 'and when he has arrived.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g56: 'comincia' is present indicative 'begins'; 'ad' (poetic form of 'a') introduces the infinitive 'operare' = 'to work/act/operate.' The gerund 'coagulando' ('coagulating'/'clotting') specifies the first operation he performs, and 'prima' (= 'first') orders the sequence. Grammatically, 'comincia ad operare coagulando prima' = 'he begins to act, first by coagulating.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g57: 'e poi' = 'and then/afterwards.' 'avviva' (from 'avvivare') is present indicative 'enlivens' / 'revives' and takes the clause 'ciò che … fé constare' as its object. 'ciò che' = 'that which,' 'per sua matera' = 'by/through its matter' (instrumental/causal), 'fé' is the past historic of 'fare' ('he made/did'), and 'constare' here means 'to make evident/manifest' or 'to stand forth.' Syntactically the clause says he first coagulates and then gives life/enlivening to what his matter had made evident.
segments.purgatorio-25-g1: Ora = 'now' (temporal adverb) sets the time. era is the imperfect/indicative 'was' describing that time. onde is a relative/adverbial 'from/at which' linking the time to the following clause. ’l (elided il) is the definite article and salir is an infinitive used as a noun 'the ascent/going up'. non volea = volea is an archaic imperfect of volere 'was/would not want', negated by non; storpio is an adjective 'lame/impeded' modifying the idea of the ascent.
segments.purgatorio-25-g2: ché = 'for/because' introduces the explanatory causal clause. ’l sole avëa uses avëa (imperfect 'had') to place the sun's position in the past. il cerchio di merigge = 'the circle/point of midday' (merigge = noon/meridian). lasciato al Tauro e la notte a lo Scorpio uses lasciato (past participle) with the sense 'left/handed over' and assigns the zodiacal positions (Tauro, Scorpio).
segments.purgatorio-25-g3: per che = 'therefore' (consequence connector) introduces the concluding consequence of the celestial timing just given.
segments.purgatorio-25-g4: come fa l’uom = 'as a man does'; fa is present indicative of fare. che non s’affigge is a relative clause: non + s’affigge (reflexive) here means 'does not fix/attach himself' (i.e. does not stay put). ma vassi a la via sua uses an archaic form vassi (here meaning 'goes on his way'); a la via sua = 'on his own way'. Word order is typical: main simile introduced by come and followed by the characterizing clause.
segments.purgatorio-25-g5: che che = an intensifying double 'whatever/whatever it be that'; li is an indirect object pronoun 'to him' (archaic placement); appaia is a subjunctive form (present) used to express potentiality 'may appear/seem' (here the lexeme is irregular/poetic).
segments.purgatorio-25-g6: se = 'if' (conditional); di bisogno = 'by/through necessity' (expressing motive); stimolo is the noun 'a spur/goad'; il is the direct object pronoun/article depending on reading; trafigge is present indicative 'pierces/transfixes' showing that a need may impel the man to act. The clause gives the exception that would force him to change course.
segments.purgatorio-25-g7: così intrammo noi = 'thus we entered'; intrammo is passato remoto of intrare 'entered' and noi is the subject pronoun (emphatic). per la callaia = 'through the callaia' (a local/poetic topographical term).
segments.purgatorio-25-g8: uno innanzi altro = 'one before another' (phrase indicating single file). prendendo la scala = gerund 'taking the ladder' (prendendo describes how they proceeded). che per artezza i salitor dispaia is a relative clause: che links to scala, per artezza expresses cause or characteristic (here 'because of its height/steepness'), i salitor 'the climbers' and dispaia (subjunctive/poetic present) means 'repels/disconcerts' (makes them fail).
segments.purgatorio-25-g9: E quale = 'and like/just as'; il cicognin is a diminutive noun 'the little/young stork' used as the simile's image. che leva l’ala = relative clause 'who lifts its wing'; leva is present indicative of levare 'raises/lifts'. per voglia di volare = 'from a desire to fly' where per + noun expresses motive.
segments.purgatorio-25-g10: e non s’attenta = 'and does not attempt/dare' (s’attenta is reflexive; here it means 'tries/ventures' rather than 'is attentive'). d’abbandonar lo nido = infinitive phrase 'to abandon the nest' (d’ = di elided). The negative non negates the whole attempt.
segments.purgatorio-25-g11: e giù la cala = 'and downwards it lowers it' where giù is an adverb 'down', la refers back to the wing, and cala is third-person present of calare 'lowers/descends'. The short clause describes the bird's hesitation.
segments.purgatorio-25-g12: tal era io = 'such was I' (predicative identification). con voglia accesa e spenta = 'with desire kindled and extinguished' (lit. 'lit and quenched'), a poetic antithesis showing alternating eagerness and hesitation. di dimandar = 'to ask' (infinitive expressing purpose/intention).
segments.purgatorio-25-g13: venendo infino a l’atto = gerund + preposition 'coming up to the act/point' (infino = 'up to'). che fa colui = 'that he who makes/does' (fa = present of fare). ch’a dicer s’argomenta = relative clause where ch’ = che 'who', a dicer = 'to say/speak', s’argomenta is reflexive 'argues with himself/hesitates' (present indicative), i.e. the behavior of someone torn about whether to speak.
segments.purgatorio-25-g14: Non lasciò is passato remoto 'did not leave/let [me]' — a concise past action; the direct object is omitted here but picked up in the next segment (lo dolce padre mio). Non negates lasciò.
segments.purgatorio-25-g15: per l’andar = 'because of the going/pace' (per = 'because of' here). che fosse ratto uses the imperfect subjunctive fosse with the predicative adjective ratto 'swift/quick', a stylistic choice to qualify the manner of his movement.
segments.purgatorio-25-g16: lo dolce padre mio = 'my sweet father' with lo as a demonstrative/article placed before the noun for emphasis; mio (possessive) follows the noun as is normal in Italian poetry and marks possession.
segments.purgatorio-25-g17: ma disse = 'but said' (ma = 'but'; disse is passato remoto of dire 'he said'), introducing the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-25-g18: Scocca is an imperative or hortative present of scoccare 'let fly/shoot' addressed to the interlocutor. l’arco del dir = 'the bow of speech' (a metaphor: arco + genitive del dir 'of saying/speech').
segments.purgatorio-25-g19: che ’nfino al ferro hai tratto = causal/explanatory clause: che = 'for', ’nfino (enfatic form of fino) = 'up to', al ferro = 'to the iron/point', hai tratto is the present perfect 'you have drawn' (hai + past participle tratto), completing the archery metaphor.
segments.purgatorio-25-g20: Allor = 'then/thereupon' (temporal connector). sicuramente is an adverb 'certainly/surely' modifying the verb. apri’ is an archaic orthography/apocope of aprii (passato remoto 'I opened') with the enclitic apostrophe; la bocca = 'the mouth' (object). e cominciai = 'and I began' (cominciai passato remoto).
segments.purgatorio-25-g21: Come si può far magro = impersonal question 'How can one be made thin' where si + può expresses a general ability/possibility and far (infinitive) means 'to make'. là dove = 'where/wherever'. l’uopo di nodrir non tocca = 'the need/necessity to nourish does not reach' — l’uopo 'the need', di nodrir (infinitive) 'to nourish', non tocca (present) 'does not touch/reach'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g85: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' Perché here introduces a purpose clause meaning 'so that' (not 'why'), and it governs the present subjunctive ammiri ('you marvel'), with meno modifying ammiri ('marvel less'). La parola is the direct object 'the word/the saying.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g86: Guarda is a 2nd-person singular imperative 'look/see.' Il calor del sole is the noun phrase 'the heat of the sun.' The relative che introduces a clause (the heat 'which') and si fa is reflexive/impersonal with fare used in the sense 'become' (si fa vino = 'turns into wine').
segments.purgatorio-25-g87: Giunto is a past participle used adverbially ('having arrived' / 'when it reaches'); giunto a introduces the point reached. L’omor is an archaic word for bodily moisture/sap, and cola is a present form meaning 'drips/flows' (what runs from the vine).
segments.purgatorio-25-g88: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when.' Làchesis is the proper name (one of the Fates). Non ha più del lino means 'no longer has the (linen/thread).', with più used in the sense 'any longer.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g89: Solvesi is solvere with the reflexive pronoun si, used here in a passive/intransitive sense 'is loosened/unwraps' or 'is released'; da la carne specifies from what it is loosened ('from the flesh').
segments.purgatorio-25-g90: E in virtute introduces the means/quality ('and by/ in virtue'), ne is a clitic referring back to the separation and signals 'of it / from there' in the phrase ne porta seco ('carries with it'); porta seco is a fixed phrase 'carry with oneself.' The correlative e l’umano e 'l divino lists the two things carried: the human and the divine.
segments.purgatorio-25-g91: L’altre potenze 'the other powers' names the remaining faculties; tutte quante is a pleonastic intensifier meaning 'all of them,' and mute is a nominative plural adjective agreeing with potenze meaning 'silent' or 'made mute.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g92: Memoria is a nominative noun in a loose appositive list of faculties (memory). It stands alone here as the first of the listed faculties.
segments.purgatorio-25-g93: Intelligenza e volontade are the next items of the list ('intellect and will'). In atto means 'in act' (actualized rather than merely potential). Molto più che prima is a comparative 'much more than before,' and agute (plural feminine) predicates 'sharp/keen,' agreeing with the listed faculties.
segments.purgatorio-25-g94: Sanza is an archaic form of senza ('without'). Restarsi is the reflexive verb 'to remain/linger' used here negated by sanza to mean 'without lingering' or 'without delay.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g95: Per sé stessa is a reflexive intensifier 'by itself / of its own accord'; cade is present indicative 'falls' describing movement; mirabilmente is an adverb 'wonderfully'; a l’una de le rive means 'to one of the banks/shores,' with l'una indicating a single bank among them.
segments.purgatorio-25-g96: Quivi 'there' marks the location; conosce is present indicative 'knows' or 'recognizes'; prima here is an adverb 'first' (temporal/ordering); le sue strade 'its paths/ways' is the direct object.
segments.purgatorio-25-g97: Tosto che is a temporal conjunction 'as soon as.' Loco lì 'that place there' is the subject/locative, and la is the direct object pronoun referring back to the thing being enclosed; circunscrive is present indicative 'circumscribes/encloses' (here 'encloses it').
segments.purgatorio-25-g98: La virtù formativa names the formative power/faculty. Raggia is used as a verb derived from raggio — 'sends a ray' or 'radiates' — and intorno 'around' indicates surrounding motion. Così e quanto is an idiom meaning 'so and as much' (giving extent), and ne le membra vive means 'into the living members,' with ne functioning as a locative/clitic referring into them.
segments.purgatorio-25-g22: This segment is punctuation only: a full stop (or pause) separating speeches/units. No lexical items to analyze; it functions to mark the end of the previous utterance.
segments.purgatorio-25-g23: Se t’ammentassi = conditional/hypothetical clause. 'Se' introduces an if-clause (here conditional). 't’' is the clitic indirect object 'to you' attached to the verb. 'ammentassi' is the imperfect subjunctive (or conditional-provisional mood in subordinate clause) of 'ammentare/ammentarsi' meaning 'to call to mind / remind / mention'; here it gives a hypothetical 'if I reminded you'. 'come' introduces the reported manner/way clause ('how'). 'Meleagro si consumò' is a reflexive past event: 'si' is reflexive and 'consumò' is passato remoto (he was consumed/consumed himself). 'al consumar d’un stizzo' uses 'al' (a + il) = 'at the', with infinitival noun 'consumar' used substantively 'the burning/consuming' and 'd’un stizzo' = 'of a brand/ember'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g24: 'non fora' is an archaic/poetic conditional form meaning 'it would not be' (equivalent to modern 'non sarebbe'). Here it closes the hypothetical begun with 'Se...': 'it would not be so'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g25: 'disse' is the passato remoto (simple past) of 'dire' and attributes the spoken words to the speaker ('he said'). It functions as the reporting verb between quoted phrases.
segments.purgatorio-25-g26: This is the continuation of the reported speech: 'a te' is the prepositional phrase 'to you' (indirect object); 'questo' is the demonstrative subject 'this'; 'sì agro' is a predicative adjective phrase 'so bitter/so harsh' with 'sì' = 'so' modifying the adjective 'agro'. Word order is inverted for emphasis (object/phrase before predicate).
segments.purgatorio-25-g27: 'e se pensassi come,' = 'and if you were to think how...' The conjunction 'e' links to the previous clause; 'se' again introduces a conditional; 'pensassi' is the imperfect subjunctive used for a hypothetical 'if you thought/were to think'; 'come' introduces the manner clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-25-g28: 'al vostro guizzo' is a prepositional phrase: 'al' = a + il 'at/to the', 'vostro' = your (plural/formal), 'guizzo' = sudden flash/darting movement. The phrase sets the location/instant ('at your flash') for the following action.
segments.purgatorio-25-g29: 'guizza dentro a lo specchio vostra image' is a compact clause with verb first: 'guizza' (3rd person singular present) = 'darts/flashes'; 'dentro a lo specchio' = 'within/in the mirror' (prepositional phrase; note the article 'lo'); 'vostra image' is the subject 'your image/reflection' placed after the locative phrase for poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-25-g30: 'ciò che par duro ti parrebbe vizzo' = 'that which now seems hard/harsh to you would appear a vizzo (vice/fault).' 'Ciò che' is a relative nominal clause 'that which'; 'par' is present 'seems'; 'duro' is the adjective 'hard/harsh'; then the main result clause 'ti parrebbe vizzo' uses the conditional 'parrebbe' ('would seem') with indirect object 'ti' ('to you') and predicate noun 'vizzo' ('a fault/vice').
segments.purgatorio-25-g31: 'Ma' = 'but'. 'perché' here gives the causal sense 'because'. 'dentro a tuo voler' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'within your will/wish' (literally 'inside your will'); 't’' is the clitic 'to you'; 'adage' is an archaic/poetic form (here attached to the clitic) meaning something like 'it is fitting / it rests / it is appropriate' — an old verbal form whose precise modern equivalent would be 'ti conviene' or 'si addice a te'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g32: 'ecco qui Stazio;' is an interjectional presentation: 'ecco' = 'behold/here is', 'qui' emphasizes location 'here', and 'Stazio' is the proper name being presented (vocative in sense).
segments.purgatorio-25-g33: 'e io lui chiamo e prego che sia or sanator de le tue piage' = 'and I call him and beseech/pray that he now be the healer of your wounds.' 'io' is the subject 'I'; 'lui' is the object 'him' (the person called); 'chiamo e prego' are two coordinated verbs 'I call and I beg/entreat'; 'che sia' introduces a subordinate clause and uses the subjunctive 'sia' to express the speaker's petition/wish; 'or' = 'now'; 'sanator' is a substantive 'healer/restorer'; 'de le tue piage' = 'of your wounds' (de = of).
segments.purgatorio-25-g34: 'Se la veduta etterna li dislego' = a conditional clause. 'Se' = if; 'la veduta etterna' = 'the eternal sight/vision' (object); 'li' is an old clitic meaning 'from him' / 'for him' (indirect/object clitic); 'dislego' is 1st person singular present of the compound verb 'dislegare' meaning 'I unbind / I free / I loosen'. The phrasing is archaic/poetic but functions as 'if I free him from the eternal vision' or 'if I unbind the eternal sight from him.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g35: 'rispuose Stazio' = past narrative: 'rispuose' is passato remoto of 'rispondere' meaning 'answered', and 'Stazio' is the speaker's name (subject).
segments.purgatorio-25-g36: 'là dove tu sie' = 'there where you are.' 'là' is the adverb 'there'; 'dove' introduces the relative locative 'where'; 'tu sie' is an archaic form of 'tu sei' (you are), using 'sie' as an older form of the verb 'to be'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g37: This clause is compact and uses archaic inflections: 'discolpi me' can be read as a subjunctive/optative or as a clause meaning 'if it absolves/excuses me' ('discolpare' = to excuse/absolve). 'non potert’ io far nego' = 'I shall not be able to make (do) denial' — literally 'I cannot make a refusal/deny.' 'potert’' is an archaic/poetic future or abilitative form of 'potere' (to be able), 'far' = 'to do/make', and 'nego' = 'denial/refusal' (or the verb 'to deny' used nominally). The whole expresses that, if the eternal sight frees or absolves him where Dante is, Statius cannot possibly deny or refuse (i.e., he will be compelled/excused).
segments.purgatorio-25-g114: This is a poetically inverted pluperfect: venuto (past participle) appears before the auxiliary s’era (si + era). The construction si era venuto a + noun is impersonal/reflective and here means “had come upon/been reached by us” (per noi = “for us”). Già is a temporal adverb “already.”
segments.purgatorio-25-g115: vòlto is a past participle/adjective meaning “turned” (here describing direction); the phrase a la man destra = “to the right hand/side.” The initial e links this clause to the previous one; la + man (mano) + destra is a normal noun phrase ‘the right hand/side.’
segments.purgatorio-25-g116: eravamo is imperfect indicative “we were”; attenti is a predicative adjective “attentive/watchful.” Ad altra cura = “to/for another care/task,” where ad introduces the object of their attention. Ed is simply e before a vowel.
segments.purgatorio-25-g117: Quivi = “there.” The clause lacks an explicit verb for the bank (a typical poetic ellipsis) and reads as a vivid participial/nominal image: “la ripa fiamma in fuor balestra” = the bank (la ripa) (sent forth) a flame outward (in fuor) like a crossbow (balestra). balestra is a simile (like a bolt/shot).
segments.purgatorio-25-g118: spira is a verb here (from spirare) “breathes/exhales”; fiato is the thing breathed (“a breath/blast”); in suso = “upward.” The relative che introduces what that upward blast does: it (la = the blast/flame) reflette (reflects) and via da lei sequestra (sequestra here = takes away/separates away from it).
segments.purgatorio-25-g119: ond’ (a contracted form of onde/ond) = “whence/therefore/so,” introducing the consequence. ir ne convenia is an impersonal/archaic phrasing meaning “it was necessary to go (ne = from there/of it),” with convenia in the imperfect used impersonally. dal lato schiuso = “from the opened side,” and ad uno ad uno = “one by one.”
segments.purgatorio-25-g120: temëa is imperfect “I feared/dreaded.” The phrase quinci (= quinci) is a locative/adverb “from here/on this side,” so io temëa ’l foco quinci = “I feared the fire on this side.” The clitic l’ = the before foco.
segments.purgatorio-25-g121: quindi here = “from there/on the other side” (a contrast to quinci). temeva (same verb, imperfect) is repeated for the opposite fear: temeva cader giuso = “feared falling down.” Cader is the infinitive following temeva; giuso = “down.”
segments.purgatorio-25-g122: Lo duca mio = “my duke/guide,” with the article lo used in Tuscan/poetic register before certain masculine nouns; dicea is imperfect “was saying.” The sequence simply introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-25-g123: si vuol tenere is an impersonal construction: si vuol = “one must/it is required,” with tenere “to keep/hold.” a li occhi stretto il freno literally “the rein tight to the eyes” = keep a tight rein on the eyes (i.e., keep one’s gaze strictly controlled). Per questo loco = “in/for this place.”
segments.purgatorio-25-g124: però ch’ = an idiomatic/concessive-causal turn meaning “because/for that” (common in older Italian). errar = infinitive “to err/stray,” and potrebbesi is a conditional impersonal form “one could/might” (potrebbe + si). per poco = “for a little / for a short time” = “for a moment.”
segments.purgatorio-25-g125: This reports a song-title or first words in Latin, Summae Deus clementiae, followed by the circumstance: nel seno al grande ardore = “in the breast/heart to (or of) the great ardor” (a poetic locution), allora = “then,” and udi’ (udi’) = archaic past “I heard” (perfect). cantando is a gerund “chanting/being chanted.”
segments.purgatorio-25-g126: che introduces a relative clause referring to the ardor/song: di volger mi fé = “made me turn” (fé = fece, causative past), caler non meno = “to descend no less” (caler = poetic infinitive ‘to go down’). The sequence expresses that the song/ardor forced the speaker both to turn and to descend.
segments.purgatorio-25-g127: vidi = perfect “I saw.” spirti (spiriti) = “spirits/souls”; per la fiamma andando = “going through the flame” (per = through, andando = gerund “going/passing”). The clause simply states what he saw while descending.
segments.purgatorio-25-g128: per ch’io = “so that I / therefore I” (introducing the reason); guardava is imperfect “I watched/kept watch,” and a loro e a’ miei passi indicates two objects watched: them and his own steps. compartendo la vista = “sharing/dividing my sight” (i.e., splitting attention), a quando a quando = “now and then / from time to time.”
segments.purgatorio-25-g99: E come = coordinating conjunction + comparative 'as'; l’aere is the elided article + noun (l' = il before a vowel). This is the protasis of the simile, with 'aere' as the subject of what follows.
segments.purgatorio-25-g100:
quand' è ben pïorno: 'quand
't (quando) è' = 'when it is'; 'ben' is an adverb modifying the following adjective/participle 'pïorno'. 'Pïorno' is an archaic/poetic form used adjectivally (past-participial sense: 'thoroughly so/filled/affected').
segments.purgatorio-25-g101: per l’altrui raggio = 'by/through another's ray' (per + article + possessive 'altrui'); che ’n sé si reflette is a restrictive relative clause: 'which reflects in itself', with 'sé' reflexive and 'si reflette' present indicative meaning physical reflection of light.
segments.purgatorio-25-g102: di diversi color = 'with/dressed in different colours' (di + plural adjective + noun); diventa addorno is a two-word predicate: present tense 'becomes' + adjectival/predicative 'adorned'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g103: così l’aere vicin quivi si mette = 'thus the air close by there puts/places itself': 'così' resumes the simile; 'vicin quivi' are adverbials ('near, there'); 'si mette' is reflexive present 'sets itself' or 'takes its place'. The following clause 'e in quella forma ch’è in lui suggella virtüalmente l’alma che ristette' has inverted order: verb 'suggella' ('seals') takes 'l'alma' as subject, preceded by the adverb 'virtüalmente', and the relative 'che ristette' ('which remained') modifies 'alma'; 'ch’è in lui' ('that is in it') modifies 'forma'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g104: e simigliante poi a la fiammella = 'and, similar then to the little flame': 'simigliante' introduces the second part of the comparison; 'poi' is temporal/continuative. The relative clause 'che segue il foco là ’vunque si muta' = 'which follows the fire wherever it changes', with 'là vunque' = 'wherever/anywhere' and 'si muta' reflexive present 'changes/transposes'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g105: segue lo spirto sua forma novella = 'the spirit follows its new form': 'segue' present indicative 'follows'; 'lo spirto' is the subject; 'sua forma novella' is the object phrase (possessive + noun + adjective). Note that 'novella' here functions adjectivally ('new'/'novel'), not as 'news' or 'tale'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g106: Però che = causal 'because/for'; 'quindi' refers back to the previously described effect; 'ha poscia sua paruta' uses avere + past participle 'paruta' (paruto) to mean 'has thereafter its appearance/has appeared', with 'poscia' = 'afterwards'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g107: è chiamata ombra = passive present 'is called/ named a shade'; straightforward copula + past participle used as passive predicate.
segments.purgatorio-25-g108: e quindi organa poi ciascun sentire infino a la veduta = 'and therefore (from that) the organs then — each sense up to sight': 'organa' (plural) means bodily or perceptive 'organs' here; 'ciascun sentire' = 'each sense/perception'; 'infino a la veduta' = 'as far as sight' (sight = highest sense). The clause is elliptical: 'are formed' or 'derive' is implied from the previous clause.
segments.purgatorio-25-g109: Quindi parliamo e quindi ridiam noi = 'So we speak and so let us laugh': 'parliamo' is present indicative 'we speak' and 'ridiam' is first-person-plural hortative ('let us laugh') or present subjunctive with hortatory force; 'noi' emphasizes the subject.
segments.purgatorio-25-g110: quindi facciam le lagrime e ’ sospiri = hortatory 'let us make/ shed the tears and the sighs'; the relative clause 'che per lo monte aver sentiti puoi' follows the object: poetic inversion places the perfect infinitive 'aver sentiti' before 'puoi' ('you can/have been able'), a common Dantean word order meaning 'that you have heard through the mountain'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g111: Secondo che = 'according to / insofar as'; 'ci affliggono' = 'afflict us' (present indicative, plural verb agreeing with 'disiri'); 'i disiri e li altri affetti' lists the causes: 'desires and the other affections/passions'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g112: l’ombra si figura = 'the shade figures itself / takes shape': 'si figura' is reflexive and here means 'assumes form' or 'is formed'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g113: e quest’ è la cagion di che tu miri = 'and this is the cause/reason that you behold': 'quest’ è la cagion' is a straightforward identification, and 'di che tu miri' is a relative/explanatory clause meaning 'why/for which you are looking/seeing'. 'Miri' is present subjunctive/indicative used here with the sense 'gaze/see'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g129: Appresso = an adverb meaning 'shortly after' or 'afterwards.' The noun phrase il fine 'the end' is followed by a relative temporal clause introduced by ch’ (che). a quell' inno fassi is an older, passive/impersonal use of farsi (fare) with si: fassi here should be read as 'was made' or 'took place' (subjunctive/passive coloring common in Dante) — literally 'that was made at/to that hymn,' i.e. 'when that hymn ended.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g130: gridavano is the imperfect (3rd person plural) of gridare, indicating ongoing or repeated shouting in the past: 'they shouted/cryed out.' alto here qualifies the manner (literally 'high'), used adverbially to mean 'aloud' or 'in a loud/high voice.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g131: This is a direct quotation in Latin: Virum (accusative) non (negation) cognosco (1st person singular present indicative of Latin cognoscō). In context the crowd shouts the phrase; grammatically it is a simple statement 'I do not know the man.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g132: indi is a temporal adverb 'then' or 'thereupon' (here the provided senses list is incorrect for context). ricominciavan is the imperfect (3rd person plural) of ricominciare 'to begin again, resume,' so 'they resumed/began again.' l' inno bassi = 'the hymn in low [voices]': bassi (plural) qualifies the manner (low/soft).
segments.purgatorio-25-g133: Finitolo is an enclitic/clitic form: finito + lo, meaning 'finished it' or in context 'when he/it had finished it.' In Dante such fused forms often stand alone as a temporal/absolute clause ('having finished it' / 'when it was finished').
segments.purgatorio-25-g134: anco is an archaic form of modern anche meaning 'also' or 'again.' gridavano is the imperfect (3rd person plural) 'they shouted/cried out.' Together: 'they also shouted.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g135: Al bosco = 'to/into the wood' (al = a + il). si tenne is reflexive: tenere with si here means 'betook herself/held herself' and in context means 'Diana withdrew/retired' to the wood; tenne is a past form (perfect/simple past usage in Dante). Diana is the proper name (goddess).
segments.purgatorio-25-g136: ed = 'and.' Elice is a proper name here (a female figure). caccionne (variant spelling of canzoni/canzone) = 'ballads' or 'long songs' (plural) — an old or dialectal form. che di Venere avea sentito il tòsco is a relative clause: che = 'which/that,' di Venere = 'of Venus,' avea sentito = pluperfect/imperfect 'had heard' (avea + past participle), il tòsco is obscure in modern sense (see senses); contextually it names a type or strain associated with Venus. Grammatically the clause modifies caccionne (the songs that had heard/of Venus had heard).
segments.purgatorio-25-g137: Indi (then) marks sequence. al cantar = 'to the singing/the song' (al = a + il). tornavano is the imperfect (3rd person plural) of tornare: 'they returned' or 'they resumed' (here: resumed the singing).
segments.purgatorio-25-g138: The second indi again means 'then' (the provided senses list in the data is wrong for the adverb). donne gridavano e mariti = 'women cried out and husbands [cried out].' The clause che fuor casti modifies those people: che = 'who/which,' fuor (here an archaic/contracted form of furon/ furono) = 'were' (past tense), casti = 'chaste.' come virtute e matrimonio imponne is a comparative/explanatory clause: 'as virtue and marriage imposed/required' — imponne is a past form 'imposed' (3rd person singular) referring back to what virtue and marriage demand.
segments.purgatorio-25-g139: E = 'and.' questo modo = 'this way/method.' credo = 1st person present 'I believe.' che introduces a subordinate clause: lor (to them/them) + basti: basti here is the present subjunctive form of bastare used after credo (credo che ... basti) meaning 'I believe that ... is sufficient for them.' per tutto il tempo che 'for the whole time that' is a temporal clause; ’l foco li abbruscia = 'the fire scorches them' (abbruscia = present or habitual sense in Dante; li as object 'them'). Note: the provided senses list has mismatches for basti and li, so those are marked accordingly.
segments.purgatorio-25-g140: con tal cura = 'with such care.' conviene is an impersonal/3rd-person singular verb meaning 'it is fitting/necessary' or 'is appropriate.' e con tai pasti = 'and with such meals' (tai = such, pasti = meals/food). che introduces a result or explanatory clause: 'that the wound (la piaga) da sezzo si ricuscia.' da sezzo is idiomatic/archaic (sezzo here conveys immediacy/entirety — in context 'at once' or 'from the very outset'), si ricuscia is reflexive/passive-like and here means 'is re-sewn/mended/heals up again' (ricuscia is an older form meaning to 'sew up again' or 'close again').
segments.purgatorio-25-g58: 'Anima' is the noun 'soul' (nominative). 'fatta' is a past participle agreeing with or predicated of 'anima' meaning 'made' or 'having become'; it introduces the complement 'la virtute attiva' (the active virtue). 'qual d'una pianta' is a simile modifying 'virtute' ('such as/of a plant') — note the compressed word order typical of poetry.
segments.purgatorio-25-g59: 'in tanto differente' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in so great a difference' / 'so different'. 'in' is the preposition, 'tanto' intensifies 'differente' (so much/so), and the adjective 'differente' follows the quantifier.
segments.purgatorio-25-g60: The conjunction 'che' introduces a result/explanatory clause. 'questa' and 'quella' are demonstrative pronouns ('this one' / 'that one'); 'è in via' = 'is on the way' (still developing) and 'è già a riva' = 'is already at the shore' (already arrived/mature). Word order is standard: pronoun — verb — locative phrase.
segments.purgatorio-25-g61: Here 'tanto' is an adverb ('so much'), and 'ovra' is an archaic form meaning 'works / acts' (3rd‑person present), so the phrase means 'then it works so much'. 'poi' is a temporal adverb 'then/afterwards'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g62: 'che' again introduces the result clause: 'that already it moves and feels.' 'già' = 'already'; 'si' is the reflexive pronoun with the verbs 'move' and 'sente' (present indicative, 3rd sing.), indicating the subject performs these actions on/for itself.
segments.purgatorio-25-g63: 'come' introduces a simile ('like'). 'spungo marino' = 'sea‑sponge' (spungo is an archaic spelling of 'spugna' / sponge); adjective 'marino' follows the noun as in Italian poetry.
segments.purgatorio-25-g64: 'e indi imprende' = 'and then it takes up / begins' — 'imprende' (from 'imprendere') is present indicative or presentive with the sense 'begins/undertakes'. 'ad organar' is the infinitive 'to organize/arrange'. 'le posse' = 'the powers' (an older plural noun meaning faculties/powers) is the direct object. 'ond’ è semente' is a relative clause 'of which it is seed' (ond' = 'whence/of which'), linking the powers to the seed/source.
segments.purgatorio-25-g65: 'Or si spiega' = 'Now it unfolds/is displayed.' 'Or' is temporal 'now'; 'si spiega' is a reflexive/passive construction of 'spiegare' meaning 'is unfolded' or 'is explained/displayed' (3rd singular).
segments.purgatorio-25-g66: 'figliuolo' is a vocative ('my son'), set off as direct address; archaic spelling for 'figliolo/figliulo'.
segments.purgatorio-25-g67: 'or si distende la virtù' — 'now the virtue stretches itself': 'si distende' is reflexive present ('extends/stretches out'); 'la virtù' is the subject ('the power/virtue'). 'ch’è dal cor del generante' is a relative clause: 'which is from the heart of the begetter' ('dal' = 'from the').
segments.purgatorio-25-g68: 'dove' introduces a locative/explanatory clause ('where'). 'natura' is the agent ('Nature'); 'a tutte membra intende' means 'attends to / directs itself to all the members' — 'intende' here = 'attends to/looks after' (present indicative). Word order is subject (natura) + indirect object (a tutte membra) + verb (intende).
segments.purgatorio-25-g69: 'Ma come d’animal divegna fante' = 'But how, from an animal, it becomes a servant.' 'Ma' = 'but'; 'come' = 'how/as'; 'd’animal' = 'from an animal'; 'divegna' is an archaic present of 'diventare' = 'becomes'; 'fante' is nominative meaning 'servant' (archaic) or 'one in servitude.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g70: 'non vedi tu ancor:' is a direct question in negative form: 'do you not yet see?' 'non' negates, 'vedi' is 2nd person present indicative (or interrogative), 'tu' is the subject pronoun for emphasis, 'ancor' = 'yet/still.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g71: 'quest’ è tal punto' = 'this is such a point/moment.' 'quest’' (questo) is the demonstrative subject, 'è' copula, 'tal' = 'such', 'punto' = 'point/moment/degree.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g72: 'che più savio di te fé già errante' — 'that one wiser than you had already made (it) errant.' 'che' introduces the clause; 'più savio di te' is a comparative noun phrase 'one wiser than you'; 'fé' is the past form 'fece' (archaic spelling) meaning 'made/caused'; 'già errante' = 'already wandering/astray' functions as object/predicate.
segments.purgatorio-25-g73: 'sì che per sua dottrina fé disgiunto' = 'so that, by his teaching, he caused [it] to be separated.' 'sì che' = 'so that' (result clause). 'per sua dottrina' = 'through/by his doctrine/teaching'; 'fé disgiunto' = 'caused to be separated' (fé + past participle 'disgiunto' forms a causative past).
segments.purgatorio-25-g74: 'perché da lui non vide organo assunto.' — 'because from him it did not see an assumed organ.' 'perché' = 'because'; 'da lui' = 'from him'; 'non vide' is past tense (vide = sees/saw) meaning 'did not see/perceive'; 'organo assunto' = 'an assumed/taken organ/instrument' (organo = instrument/organ; assunto = assumed/taken on).
segments.purgatorio-25-g75: 'Apri' is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'Open!'; 'a la verità' is the indirect object 'to the truth'; 'che viene il petto' is a relative clause 'that comes to the breast' (i.e. that comes into the breast). The sequence addresses the listener and commands openness to the truth.
segments.purgatorio-25-g76: 'e sappi che,' — 'and know that...' 'sappi' is the 2nd‑person singular imperative of 'sapere' ('know!'), introducing the truth that follows; 'che' introduces the content clause.
segments.purgatorio-25-g77: 'sì tosto come' is a temporal conjunction 'as soon as.' 'al feto' = 'to/for the fetus'; 'l’articular del cerebro' = 'the articulation (structure) of the brain' (poetic/archaic noun), and 'è perfetto' = 'is perfected/complete.' Word order places the time clause first.
segments.purgatorio-25-g78: 'lo motor primo a lui si volge lieto' — 'the first mover turns himself to it (the fetus) gladly.' 'lo motor primo' is the subject 'the prime mover'; 'a lui' = 'to it/him'; 'si volge' is reflexive present 'turns itself/addresses itself'; 'lieto' is an adverb/adjectival complement 'gladly/favourably.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g79: 'e spira spirito novo' — 'and breathes a new spirit.' 'spira' (from 'spirare') is 3rd‑person present 'breathes/inhales/exhales/instils'; here it means 'bestows' or 'breathes into.' 'spirito novo' = 'a new spirit.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g80: 'di vertù repleto' = 'filled with virtue/power.' 'di' is the preposition, 'vertù' here = 'virtue/power/faculty', and 'repleto' is a past participle used adjectivally 'full/filled.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g81: 'che ciò che trova attivo quivi' — relative clause: 'which (i.e. that spirit) — whatever it finds active there.' 'che' introduces the clause; 'ciò che' = 'that which/whatever'; 'trova' is present 'finds'; 'attivo' = 'active'; 'quivi' = 'there.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g82: 'tira in sua sustanzia' = 'draws/pulls into its own substance.' 'tira' is present indicative 'pulls/draws'; 'in sua sustanzia' is the prepositional phrase 'into its substance/essence.'
segments.purgatorio-25-g83: 'e fassi un’alma sola' — 'and makes/ becomes a single soul.' 'fassi' is reflexive/passive of 'fare' meaning 'is made/becomes'; 'un’alma sola' = 'one single soul.' This is the result of the previous action.
segments.purgatorio-25-g84: 'che vive e sente e sé in sé rigira.' — relative clause qualifying 'alma sola': 'which lives and feels and turns itself within itself.' 'vive' and 'sente' are present verbs; 'sé in sé rigira' is a reflexive phrase meaning the soul turns/reflects itself upon itself (rigira = 'turns again/reflects').

sentences.purgatorio-25-s9: Then he began: "If my words, son, your mind attends to and receives [them], I will shed light for you on the way you mean (how you say it)."
sentences.purgatorio-25-s10: Perfect blood, which thereafter is no longer drunk by the thirsty veins and remains almost like food lifted from the table, imparts into the heart and to all human members a formative virtue, like that which, by means of its matter, brought those (members) to be made through the empty veins.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s11: Still being digested, it descends where it is better to be silent than to speak; and then afterward it laments over another’s blood in a natural vessel.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s12: There both are received together: one prepared to suffer, and the other to act through the perfect place from which it is pressed; and when he has arrived he begins to operate, first coagulating, and then quickening what by its matter he had made evident.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s1: Now it was a time when the climb was not liable to be hindered; for the sun had left the meridian circle to Taurus and the night to Scorpio; therefore, as a man who does not fix himself but goes his own way, whatever may appear to him, if a spur of necessity pierces him, thus we entered through the narrow path, one before another taking the ladder which, because of its steepness, discourages climbers.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s2: And like the young stork that lifts its wing because it wants to fly, and yet does not dare to abandon its nest, and lowers it down; such was I, with desire kindled and quenched to ask, coming up to the very point where one who hesitates about speaking reaches.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s3: He did not leave me — because his pace was quick — my sweet father, but said: "Let fly the bow of speech, for you have drawn it up to the iron."
sentences.purgatorio-25-s4: Then I certainly opened my mouth and began: "How can one be made thin where the need to nourish does not reach?"
sentences.purgatorio-25-s17: And so that you may marvel less at the saying, behold the sun’s heat which becomes wine when it reaches the sap that runs from the vine.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s18: When Lachesis no longer has the thread, it is loosed from the flesh, and by virtue it takes with it both the human and the divine: the other powers all are made mute; memory, intelligence, and will are in act much keener than before.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s19: Without lingering, it falls by itself marvelously to one of the banks; there it first knows its paths.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s20: As soon as that place there encloses it, the formative virtue radiates around so far and as much into the living members.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s6: “If I were to remind you how Meleager consumed himself in the burning of a brand, it would not, he said, be so bitter to you; and if you were to think how, at your flash, your image darts within the mirror, what now seems harsh to you would seem a vice.”
sentences.purgatorio-25-s7: “But because it lies within your will,” he continued, “behold Statius here; and I call him and beg that he now be the healer of your wounds.”
sentences.purgatorio-25-s8: “If I unloose from him the eternal vision,” answered Statius, “there where you are, if it absolves me I shall not be able to make denial.”
sentences.purgatorio-25-s25: And already the last torment had come upon us, and (it) had turned to the right hand, and we were attentive to another care.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s26: There the bank spat a flame outward like a crossbow, and the cornice breathed an upward blast that reflects it and carries it away from it; wherefore one had to go from the opened side one by one; and I feared the fire on this side, and on the other side I feared falling down.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s27: My guide was saying: “In this place one must keep a tight rein on the eyes, because one might stray/err for a little.”
sentences.purgatorio-25-s28: “Summae Deus clementiae,” I heard chanted then in the breast to the great ardor, which made me turn and descend no less; and I saw spirits going through the flame; wherefore I watched them and my steps, dividing my sight now and then.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s21: And as the air, when it is well (thoroughly) permeated, through another's ray that is reflected in itself, becomes adorned with different colours; so the air close by there sets itself, and in that form which is in it the soul that remained seals itself virtually; and, similarly to the little flame that follows the fire wherever it changes, the spirit follows its new form.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s22: Because from that it afterwards has its appearance, it is called a shade; and from that then (come) the organs — each sense up to sight.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s23: So we speak and so we laugh; so let us make the tears and the sighs that you may have heard through the mountain.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s24: According as desires and the other affections afflict us, the shade takes form; and this is the cause of what you behold.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s29: Shortly after the end that was made to that hymn, they shouted aloud: "Virum non cognosco"; then they resumed the hymn in low voices.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s30: When it was finished, they also cried out: "Diana betook herself to the wood, and Elice (sang) ballads that had heard the Tuscan note of Venus."
sentences.purgatorio-25-s31: Then they returned to the singing; then women and husbands — who were chaste, as virtue and marriage required — cried out.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s32: And I believe that this method suffices for them for the whole time that the fire scorches them: with such care and with such meals that the wound is sewn up again (heals) from the very first.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s13: When a soul makes the active virtue like that of a plant — so different that one is on the way and the other already at the shore — it then works so much that it already moves and feels like a sea‑sponge; and from there it begins to set in order the powers of which it is the seed.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s14: Now it unfolds, my son, now the virtue that is from the heart of the begetter stretches itself, where Nature directs itself to all the members.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s15: But how it becomes, from the animal, a servant — you do not yet see: this is such a point that one wiser than you had already made it errant, so that by his teaching he separated the possible intellect from the soul, because from him it did not see an assumed organ.
sentences.purgatorio-25-s16: Open your breast to the truth that comes; and know that, as soon as in the fetus the articulation of the brain is perfected, the first mover turns himself to it gladly over so great an art of nature, and breathes a new spirit, filled with virtue, which draws into its own substance whatever it finds active there, and makes a single soul, which lives and feels and turns itself within itself.
